image: epithelioid cell type and prominent nucleoli (arrow).jpg
res:
Pathologic diagnosis:
Retina, excisional biopsy

---

Microscopic findings:
Sections of the specimen show a dense infiltrate of small round to oval cells with hyperchromatic nuclei and minimal cytoplasm. There is no evidence of retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) involvement. The infiltrate is predominantly located in the subretinal space and extends into the underlying retina. The cells are negative for Melan-A, HMB45, and S-100. The infiltrate shows no significant mitotic activity. There is no evidence of vascular invasion or retinal detachment. The overall architecture of the retina appears preserved.

Based on these findings, the most likely diagnosis is:
---
Inflammatory pseudotumor (also known as granulomatous inflammation) of the retina
---

Additional comments:
The lack of RPE involvement and the absence of significant atypia or mitotic activity suggest an inflammatory process rather than a neoplastic one. The dense infiltrate could be consistent with a reaction to an infectious agent or an immune-mediated process. Further clinical correlation and ancillary studies may be necessary for a definitive diagnosis.
gts:
Pathologic diagnosis: Choroidal melanoma.
Microscopic findings: epithelioid cell type and prominent nucleoli is shown. Epithelioid melanoma cells with prominent nucleoli are present.

image: low power shows a tumor in the subretinal area.jpg
res:
Pathologic diagnosis:
Retina, excisional biopsy

Microscopic findings:
Sections of the specimen show a well-circumscribed lesion in the retina. The lesion appears to be a choroidal nevus, characterized by a proliferation of melanocytes in the choroid. The nevus cells are arranged in a nodular pattern and are surrounded by a fibrous capsule. There is no evidence of atypia, mitotic activity, or vascular invasion. The overlying retinal pigment epithelium shows some hyperplasia but no significant atrophy or dysplasia. The surrounding choroid shows mild fibrosis and pigmentation. Immunohistochemical staining for Melan-A and HMB45 confirms the presence of melanocytic cells. The lesion does not exhibit any features suggestive of malignancy such as pleomorphism, increased mitotic activity, or invasion into the subretinal space.

res:
Pathologic diagnosis:
Retina, excisional biopsy

---

Microscopic findings:
Sections of the specimen show a dense infiltrate of atypical cells with pleomorphic nuclei and prominent nucleoli. The cells are arranged in a diffuse pattern with some areas showing a nested arrangement. There is significant nuclear atypia, including hyperchromasia and irregular nuclear contours. Mitotic figures are present. The cells are positive for S-100 protein, indicating a neural origin. There is no evidence of retinal detachment or rupture of Bruch's membrane. The overall appearance is highly suggestive of a high-grade neuroendocrine tumor, possibly a small cell carcinoma or a poorly differentiated neuroendocrine tumor of the retina.

---

This report should be reviewed by a senior ophthalmic pathologist for final confirmation and grading.

---

Microscopic findings:
Sections of the specimen show a dense infiltrate of small round to oval cells with hyperchromatic nuclei and minimal cytoplasm. The cells are arranged in a diffuse pattern with some areas showing a nodular arrangement. There is no evidence of retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) involvement. The infiltrate is composed of lymphocytes and plasma cells, with occasional eosinophils. There is no evidence of retinal detachment or choroidal involvement. The infiltrate is localized to the subretinal space. Immunohistochemical staining shows positivity for CD20, highlighting the lymphoid component. No evidence of malignancy is noted in the specimen examined.

image: spindle A cell type has nuclear grooving (arrow).jpg
res:
Pathologic diagnosis:
Retina, excisional biopsy

---

Microscopic findings:
Sections of the specimen show a dense infiltrate of large atypical cells with pleomorphic nuclei and prominent nucleoli. The cells are arranged in a diffuse pattern with some areas showing a nested arrangement. There is significant nuclear pleomorphism and mitotic activity. The cells are positive for S-100 protein, indicating a neural crest origin. There is no evidence of retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) involvement. The surrounding stroma shows a chronic inflammatory infiltrate. The presence of large atypical cells with S-100 positivity suggests a diagnosis of retinoblastoma.

---

Proposed WHO grading system: High-grade retinoblastoma

res:
Pathologic diagnosis:
Retina, excisional biopsy

---

Microscopic findings:
Sections of the specimen show a dense infiltrate of small to medium-sized lymphocytes and plasma cells in the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and underlying choroid. There is no evidence of neoplastic cells. The infiltrate is predominantly perivascular and interstitial, with some areas of mild perivascular cuffing. The RPE shows mild hyperplasia and pigmentation. There is no evidence of retinal detachment or choroidal neovascularization. The overall architecture of the retina appears intact.

No evidence of malignancy is noted in the specimen examined. The histologic features are consistent with an inflammatory process, likely representing a chronic inflammatory reaction.

Microscopic findings:
Sections of the specimen show a dense infiltrate of lymphocytes and plasma cells in the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and underlying choroid. There is no evidence of neoplastic proliferation. The infiltrate is predominantly composed of CD4+ and CD8+ T-cells, with some B-cells and plasma cells. The RPE shows mild hyperplasia and focal areas of pigmentation. No evidence of melanoma or other neoplastic lesions is observed. The histologic features are consistent with an inflammatory process, likely representing a chronic inflammatory response to an underlying cause such as a viral infection or autoimmune condition.

res:

Retina, excisional biopsy

Microscopic findings:
Sections of the specimen show a dense infiltrate of lymphocytes and plasma cells in the subretinal space, with associated retinal pigment epithelial (RPE) changes. There is no evidence of a primary tumor or neoplastic process. The RPE shows mild hyperplasia and pigmentation. The underlying retina appears intact with no evidence of atrophy or necrosis. The presence of lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate suggests an inflammatory process, possibly related to a chronic or acute uveitis. No evidence of malignancy is noted in the specimen examined.

Microscopic findings:
Sections of the specimen show a dense infiltrate of lymphocytes and plasma cells in the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and underlying choroid. There is no evidence of neoplastic proliferation. The RPE shows mild hyperplasia and pigmentation. The choroid appears to be mildly reactive with some areas of fibrosis. No evidence of choroidal neovascularization or other specific lesions is noted. Immunohistochemical stains for CD3 and CD20 highlight the lymphocytic infiltrate, while stains for CD68 and S-100 are negative for macrophages and melanocytic cells, respectively.

res:
Pathologic diagnosis:
Sclera, excisional biopsy

Microscopic findings:
Sections of the specimen show a dense fibrous tissue with scattered chronic inflammatory cells, predominantly lymphocytes and plasma cells. There is no evidence of neoplastic proliferation. The collagen fibers appear thickened and arranged in a haphazard manner. No atypical cells or areas suggestive of malignancy are observed. The histologic features are consistent with chronic scleral inflammation. No evidence of malignancy is noted in the specimen examined.

res:
Pathologic diagnosis:
Retina, excisional biopsy

Microscopic findings:
Sections of the specimen show a dense infiltrate of large atypical cells with pleomorphic nuclei and prominent nucleoli, some of which have a clear cytoplasmic halo. There is significant nuclear pleomorphism and mitotic activity. The infiltrate is composed predominantly of large, round to oval cells with hyperchromatic nuclei and prominent nucleoli, some of which appear multinucleated. There is also a presence of smaller lymphocytes and plasma cells. The background shows a fibrotic stroma with scattered hemosiderin-laden macrophages. Immunohistochemical staining reveals that the atypical cells are positive for CD30 and negative for S-100, EMA, and HMB45. The overall appearance is highly suggestive of a large cell lymphoma, specifically an anaplastic large cell lymphoma (ALCL).
